Hello and welcome to American Writers and Artists Incorporated Teleconference Series. My name is Denise Ford and I'm very happy to welcome you to part two in our Fiction Nonfiction Publishing Call series. Today's call, Beyond Tradition, is discussing about new publishing options, those options that are available to writers both online and offline in today's market. We have a fascinating array of panelists joining us for today's call from various places around the country. Gary Scott, are you calling us from the Blue Ridge Mountains? Is that what I understand? I am indeed. Welcome, Gary. Thank you. Thank you for being with us. This is great to be here. Gary is both a business entrepreneur and an author. Some of you may be familiar with 65th Octave, which is an economic thriller that he's written. He's written dozens of books and courses on investing and business. He's also the editor behind AWAI's new program that Gary will talk a little bit about later. Welcome, Gary. It's a pleasure to be here. Also joining Gary is Margaret Frazier. Hello, Margaret. Hello. Thank you and good morning. Good morning. I understand that you began writing at 16. Is that true? Yes. And then you worked for a newspaper? Yes, I worked for a newspaper for a couple of years in my teens. That's the one. And it was back in the 60s, so it was an opinion column. Well, I think your opinions have turned into quite a skill at writing novels, particularly romance novels. Margaret has completed two romance suspense manuscripts and has won various contest awards, including the Orange Rose Award and first place in the Emerald City Opener and the prestigious Golden Heart Award. And today, Margaret works as both a writer and an editor for Biography for Everyone, which she will tell us about later. So thank you for joining us again, Margaret. Thank you. Also on the panel is Joe Manning. Good morning, Joe. Good morning to you all. And Joe is the author of Seducing Mr. Haywood, which was named one of the top ten novels of the year by Booklist in 2002. She is working on a novel called Courting Chloe Brown, which is a sequel. And she founded the Reader's Digest General Books Library and has been a publishing division president for many years. So we're very happy to have Joe with us this morning. And the last panelist today's call is Russell Davis. Hello, Russell. Good morning. And where are you calling us from? My truck. I'm calling from Arizona. In your truck. Well, is that because it's cooler in your truck? Most days, yes. Actually, I'm just coming back from dropping my kids to school. Oh, well, that's so nice of you to stop and take time to join us and share some information. Happy to do so. Russell is an author, editor, and a part-time agent. And he has a publishing house called Foggy Windows Books that specializes in a variety of niches, but also semi-erotic content for married couples. And I know that that has a broad appeal for writers and non-writers alike. Russell was hired by Five Star Publishing when he took over that control of those lines. And in the spring of 2004, he launched his own book packaging and editorial services company, which is Morning Storm Books. So thank you, Russell, for joining us. Glad to help. My co-host, I should say, on this romance series is Marcia King. Gamble, good morning, Marcia. Good morning, everyone. Thank you for lining up these very interesting rosters of writers. My pleasure. And the gentlemen who will guide us through today's call, where we are going to talk about the options in publishing today, is our renowned and much-loved moderator, Bob Bly. Good morning, Bob. Good morning, Denise. Thank you so much, Bob. I'm going to turn the mic over to you so you can manage all of these wonderful folks. Okay, well, we've got a lot of people on the line and a lot of subjects. So we're through with part one of our call, which is our Welcome and Introductions. So I'm going to move swiftly on to part two, which is called Discussion on the Pros and Cons of Self-Publishing and Print on Demand. And we're going to talk with Gary, Scott, and Russell Davis about self-publishing and Print on Demand. So Gary, how many books have you written in? Are they all self-published or some of them self-published or only a few? Everything except for my novel have been self-published. And I've lost count. If we talk about books and reports other than our daily easing and our monthly newsletters and weekly newsletters, we've probably produced 40 or 50 books and reports about various aspects of international investing. We started back in the 1960s, the late 1960s, and really I started as a salesman trying to sell mutual funds in Hong Kong, American Mutual Funds, which was the wrong time to do that. And I discovered over there that the Hong Kong stock market was the best place. And I was trying to figure out how to sell that idea to people back in the United States. And it turned out that the best idea, it seemed to me at that time, was to write a book about it or report. So that was my first report. And everything's just sort of kept going since then. Every place I travel, I see something interesting in the investment field mainly. And so I write reports about them. Now, why do you choose to self-publish these materials rather than go to a Charles Scribner's? For example, Andrew Hacker, I think you probably know who he is who wrote money. I met him in a bar and he mentioned his publisher is Scribner's and they do a lot of financial books. But you've chosen not to go that route. What's the reason? Profitability mainly. And my wife and I both work very well together and she was editor of several award-winning magazines in South Florida, Goof Shore Life being the main one. So we have most of the capability of doing the things we need to do at the editorial level. And we're recluses and we like to control our own destiny. And we found that when we go to a publisher, as I try to do with my novel or as I did with my novel, things get out of control. So we can really maintain our own destiny and figure out how to reach the kinds of people that we want to reach talking about interesting things from our point of view and delivering that information exactly to the kinds of people we want to do business with. I understand what you're saying. How do you sell most of your books and reports? Are they sold in a bookstore like a regular publisher would do? Or do you sell them to these people through different channels? No, not at all. Now we've totally moved to electronic media. We don't personally publish anything at all. Now we do have some publishers, AY, American Writers and Artists Institute. We've worked with in developing the self-publishing course. And we have some other publishers that are publishing some of the reports that we used to print. And we did start using the print medium. But we shifted back in 1999 with the growth of the Internet to just doing electronic things which suits us much more. We live up here in the Blue Ridge Mountains. Our nearest printer of any sort now is probably close to an hour away. So Internet fits us very nicely. But when we began, we always printed our reports, what I called in a plain Jane style, which was 8.5 by 11 with just regular typewriter fonts. So we never got into graphics. It was just information that we focused on. So Russell, are you in a similar type of business? Do you publish electronic stuff? Do you publish physical paper books? Well, now because I'm a book packageer, my principal job is to bring concepts to publishers. So what I do is I create a concept for a book series or a title. And then I take it to the publishers and sell them on the concept and hire the writer to develop it. So the contractual relationship with the publisher is with me and the writer's contract is with me. So the writer is an undercontract with the publisher directly. They're under contract with me. So if I'm a writer, should I be looking to sell my books or to get a book? If I want to get a book published, should I be looking to go to a publisher or should I look for what you're calling a book packageer and where do I find them? Well I think there's a few different issues there. One of the main ones is that different types of books or information lend themselves to different approaches. For example, what Gary was just talking about, that's a great opportunity for someone to build a business because they're able to provide a specific type of information and deliver it to a specific market in a self-publishing methodology. In the case of what I do, it's actually less conducive for a writer to come to me with a concept or a book. I'm wearing my book packaging hat as it were because ideas are cheap. I haven't run out of ideas yet. So what I tend to do is maintain a database of writers who can do specific types of work and when I have a concept that I've developed that fits a certain writer's profile, I'll come to them. So let me ask, go back to Gary. What do you reckon? Gary, you said you had all these books self-published, all these reports, except your novel. Why would that be the exception? Exactly what Russell was talking about fits him perfectly. The things that I usually come across to my travelers and for some reason I got a travel bug. I was born and raised in Portland, Oregon and never traveled until I turned 21. Once I started traveling, Mary and I haven't stopped in 40 years. So we get someplace and we see something that's going on that's really interesting to us. We think it will be really interesting to some of our readers, but it's not going to last for very long. So we'll write a report about that. We sell it at a pretty high price. Our typical reports, when they were printed, were selling at $49.79.99. And how many pages was that? Usually we calculated in marketing or selling our product at about a dollar a page. So it would be like a 50 page report or something. A 50 page report would be $49.00. A 100 page report would be $99.00. That report might not be valid for 40 years or years. So we produced that product, got it out of the marketplace and if I sold a thousand of them, we made $100,000.00, which is not bad to do 9 or 10 of those a year. With a little small operation and no overheads, you can actually start to accumulate some money. So I made a novel and one reason I decided I wanted to write a novel above and beyond it being fun was that I kind of got tired of having to come up with stuff that had to keep being renewed or kept reviewed every time you went back to the printer. And to me, a novel was something like, hey, I can just write this once and it'll be out there and we'll keep working. So that was the big difference between the novel and those little short reports. So it turned out we didn't make really very much money with the novel until the publisher that was publishing went bankrupt and we took it back over and started selling it ourselves. But we came from a sales background and that's why I think that kind of report has worked well for us. Now for the novel that you sell that not as an electronic product, you sell it as a paper book? It's paper. It's printed by a novel and distributed through new leaf distributors and sold through bookstores and so forth. Our exercise at that level was not successful or very successful and really where we recaptured our publishing expense and made a profit was going out and selling it to our own readers. Now did you sell it to them as a hard book or an electronic book? No, I sold that to them as a hard book. It's the only actual printed product that I have left that I market. If someone is going to self-publish, Gary, should they publish at the local print shop? Are there specialty book printers or what about this print on demand? Is that how you produce it today? Well yeah, there's a variety of different approaches and that depends an awful lot on the product. For example, our product which was meant to be its important information that's valuable right now looked great printed as a report on 8.5 by 11 paper and it almost looked like a typewriter was printing it. We didn't worry about fonts. We didn't worry about graphics or color. If you're printing something that has photography or something where the graphic form is a part of the function, then you may want a different kind of printer. We always used not the quick print people but we used a printer who just did plain Jane type of work. Got it. But you can if it's a traditional book find printers that specialize in printing books. Yeah, you can get printers that specialize in printing books and the print on demand is really attractive if a person has a limited budget or they're just getting started. One of the recommendations in our self publishing program is to start small and learn. There's the old saying that in education learning all the rules and experience learning all the exceptions of the rules. Every person's book and marketplace is going to be a little bit different. In AY self publishing program we try to show people how to get started small and then use what we call the evolutionary publishing cycle which is that you have an idea that leads to enthusiasm, the enthusiasm leads to education, the education leads to action and that action will give you a profit and experience and then you go through that cycle again in a bigger way. So print on demand is very attractive because you don't have a great big inventory. Most printers, the problem with the printing process is that the price drops enormously as the number of copies that are printed increase. You go to a printer and you say, well here's my book, it's 120 pages with the cost and he says, well if you want me to print 500 it'll be $9 a piece, if you want me to print 1,000 it'll be 750, if you want me to print 10,000 it'll be $4 a copy. So the temptation is to print 10,000 copies and then you have a storage situation, you have a delivery situation, you have a marketing situation and if you get your marketing wrong to begin, you have a big outlay and very often you might expend all your capital on printing the book and have no money left to correctly market your product. Now Eric, can I bounce off something Gary said? Sure, absolutely. Which is that really whether a person is looking at self publishing versus print on demand versus traditional publishing, that's a really important question that Gary brought up that they need to ask themselves which is when you self publish you're not just a writer anymore. You are also a sales and marketing guru, you are a publisher, you are a distributor, you are wearing a great many different hats, all of which require a different skill set. And it sounds to me like Gary's been very fortunate between he and his wife, they had a great many of those bases covered but an aspiring writer who's coming into this for the first time really needs to ask themselves do they or someone in their immediate circle that can help them have the skill set to handle the different tasks. So what if they don't? What should they do? Well again, I think first and foremost the question is what are they publishing? This is an aspiring novelist, they need to ask themselves what do they want out of their novel. In traditional publishing, virtually hundreds of books are published and released by various publishers of various sizes every month. Very few are money makers. For most writers the advance that they receive when they sign the contract is going to be the money they make. It's just mathematically very difficult to make a lot of money as a novelist. I remember when I first started publishing regularly that my family was surprised that I wasn't a millionaire already and the math doesn't work that way. On the other hand, I haven't had to go out and hand sell everything and as a writer when I consider what my time is and my return on investment and so on, I didn't want to spend my time dealing with sales and dealing with distribution and dealing with those things. So I chose a traditional publishing route for my novels. If somebody else is willing to and there are people actually right here in Arizona that I see at every fair and county event and so on, they're willing to go out and invest in the time and the money to hand sell their book to as many people as they can. The question I think of an aspiring writer would need to ask themselves is very simple. Where do I want to spend my time and my money? I think that's a good point and it's a good segue into our next part of the call, three which you gentlemen will continue with me. Alternative genres you may not have considered and why you might be interested. Gary, they list you as having your alternative market being the nonfiction market and specifically reports. But you never go out and hand sell as Russell's describing your reports. You do it on the Internet, don't you? Yeah. Now we do it on the Internet and prior to that, we never hand sold either. We always market it. That's why with the self-publishing program that we're coming out with, we call it a self-publishing rather than a self-writing or a writer's course and it approaches it as a sales and marketing person first and a writer second. What Russell was saying is absolutely right. There's three elements in a successful publication. You've got the writer who is the creator that wants to get a message out. You've got the editor who then has to take that and protect the reader's interest to say, well, writer, you're crazy or you're not communicating, which you're trying to say the reader will never understand it. And then you have the publisher who's on the other hand looking at the whole thing and protecting the banker's interest if you want to call it that of the financial interest and he's saying, well, you guys are a great editor. You've protected the reader or writer. You're getting this nice story about how we're going to make a buck from it. And that publishing course is aimed at that and we normally look at nonfiction. Recommend people start with nonfiction because you can tie a concrete benefit to most nonfictional things. It's about something that can help people improve their lives where with a novel there's a tendency towards moving into entertainment and it's much harder to put a specific price on entertainment. If I write a book about how to buy a house inexpensively in Ecuador, I can show people how they can save tens of thousands of dollars and so 99 bucks is not so much for a 100 page report. But my novel, which was an economic spiritual adventure, it was much harder to give people a specific benefit that you can sell from. Let me ask you another key question. In the nonfiction market where you're talking about reports, books, you don't seem to differentiate greatly between whether it's a report or a book. In other words, it seems like the format should follow the function. If it's 50 pages worth of stuff, it should be a report. If it's 200, it should maybe be a book. But is any topic fair game for nonfiction? In other words, if I want to write a special report on raising three-legged chickens and there's only 12 people in the United States interested in that, how can I make a living at it? Well, okay. First of all, I rarely, I guess I've written two books, everything else is a report because there is a perception of a book being worth less money than a report. Ten to twenty dollars. Ten to twenty dollars. Yeah, yeah, for a book. So a report can be worth a lot more and there's an implication in a report that it is useful current information. It's really up to date and really vital and something a person can use to impact their life in a very, very specific way rather than a little bit broader type of situation, an entertainment type of situation. Although some books will have information that can change a person's life dramatically, it's harder to pinpoint the specific dollar amount if you want to use that. And we even do that. We've shifted a lot now into also sharing global health secrets in our reports but we try and tie those health benefits back to specific dollar savings so a person can still see why they'll be ahead financially by paying this ridiculous price for pieces of paper. So it's that information in there that's really vital. And the way we try and help people do this so that they can mark it correctly and what the self-publishing program is all about is showing a person how to use their own, we call it turning your passion into profit, how to see your own wants, needs and desires and things that interest you represent the market. And then you follow that desire back through the marketplace. In other words, if you go fishing someplace and you really enjoy that fishing and you want to write a report about the 10 best fishing holes in the world, you would then start to look and say, well, where do I engage in fishing? I go to sporting good stores, I buy outdoor life magazine, I do this and I do that. And those are the places you would start looking at as the places where you would actually do your marketing. Got it. So a bookstore is not always the best place. If I'm selling something, a report on scuba diving, I probably want to be in the dive shop. Or advertising and marketing and dive magazines. Or advertising and dive related websites. And from a marketing point of view, we do three different things. One we place advertisements in publications that would have an interest. For example, not too long ago, now about 10 years ago, we decided to write a report on shamanism and Ecuador. And that was very, very different than what we've been publishing about. But we've been to Ecuador looking for investments to write about. And we've found these enormous health benefits and spiritual benefits and shamanism. And so we tried to figure out how are we going to do this? We can't go to our normal readers who are interested in international investing and sell them a report on shamanism. So I started thinking about where do I engage in my health things? And it was a health food store. So I went to the health food stores, looked, saw magazines that were given away in the health food store and were sold. And those are the places where we advertised and sold our reports. The second place we advertise is in websites that have some sort of similar focus. So if we were doing the shamanism thing today, we'd go into the search engines and type in shamanism and start looking for websites that had advertisements that might fit into that area of focus. And then the third thing we do is we start writing articles and sending them off to magazines, to newsletters, to anybody we can and might write or produce what we've written, something that's short, something that's interesting, and mention our website or mention our product. Got it. Now Russell, your market is not nonfiction. And specifically, erotica. Is erotica porn or is it something different? Well, you know, that's a good question. A lot of people, you know, will have trouble differentiating between the two. But I think that maybe the best way to think of it is that erotica is a storyline where the sex serves the storyline. In the case of porn, there's no problem with the storyline except to produce the sex. When you're dealing with relationships, a natural outgrowth of intimate relationships between people is sex. And there's a rapidly growing marketplace there, both in traditional publishing and what I'll call non-traditional publishing, for stories that are explicit in terms of the sexual content, but also that are still good stories. So are these novels or these short stories and where do I find these markets? Because I don't even know what you really mean by a non-traditional market. So if I want to write erotica, am I writing long format or short format, novel or short story, and who am I telling this to? Well, first of all, for the sake of our conversation, let's differentiate how I would deem traditional versus non-traditional publishing. I'd say traditional publishing, what I'm referring to are the vast majority of large and mid-sized publishers whose books go out to the bookstores. When I say non-traditional, I'm referring to a rather large umbrella of possibilities ranging from electronic books to smaller houses and regional presses. And even self-publishing and avenues of that sort. A couple of years ago, a really amazing thing happened with a company called Alora's Cave. Alora's Cave is an erotica publisher. They started off doing e-books. And they rapidly built a huge market of people who were willing to pay a significant premium to read erotica, both short stories and novels. And more recently, they've branched out now some of their books are available as trade paperbacks. And a number of their authors have even made the leap from doing publishing with Alora's Cave to publishing with traditional publishers in New York. And more and more traditional publishers in New York are starting lines of books that publish erotica. So, in 30 seconds or less, best way to break into erotica? Read a lot, write it well. And is there a directory of erotica publishers or a literary agent that specializes in erotica? Actually, the Internet is an amazing thing. You can go to Google and type in erotic literature or erotica and find virtually hundreds of resources, both publishers, agents, electronic publishers, all of whom do that. Terrific. Let me move to Margaret now. Margaret, welcome. Hello. Hi. And you, Margaret, you specialize in biographies. Is that it? Yes, I work as a writer and an editor for a company called Biography for Everyone. It's biographyforeveryone.com. And we write and publish, we're a full service company and we put out books for people basically who are just really trying to leave a legacy for their family and friends, not, you know, a huge print run on these books. They're just usually, you know, a few hundred to give out as gifts to their family and friends and leave a legacy of their life. So you produce the whole book for them, is that it? Yes, the company does. I work as a writer and an editor and we have professional interviewers, transcribers, writers, editors, graphic design artists, proofreaders, formats. It's a whole full service company. I don't think a lot of the Vanity Presses in general are this way, but we've been business for 18 years and offer, you know, quite a full service. Now can you define for the listeners what a Vanity Press is? My understanding is that a Vanity Press is a publisher that publishes a book at the expense of the author. So why would I want to go to a Vanity Press rather than, let's say, McGraw Hill or Random House? Well, I think in the case of what I'm doing with someone who is not wanting distribution or a large print run, that a Vanity Press works really well, particularly if they need a lot of help with what they're doing for a memoir or a biography or something like that, actually personally also as a romance writer and a novelist I would not suggest Vanity Press or that sort of a book because you want the distribution and the marketing and you want to be in the books. So a Vanity Press is good for a book that a traditional publisher may not or probably would not take and one that is not written primarily for profit as a motive? Correct. Okay. That has a limited distribution. Right. Got it. Now moving on, Joe, you also do biographies. Is that it? This is a new thing for me. I started out writing fiction. I wrote a book called The Reluctant Guardian. Right. And then for a private publisher she soon went out of business unfortunately, but I got good reviews on the book. And I did a sequel called Seducing Mr. Heywood, which did really well for me. I did that through Five Star, which was a company that Russell was once associated with. And that had several incarnations. It came out in hardcover, trade paper from Five Star, both of them. And then it was marketed as Mass Market last year by NAL. So it's had a good life and I'm very happy with it. I'm currently working on a sequel to that. It's a trilogy of stories about the same family in England in the 18th century, around the time of the Regency. My interest in the Regency period led me to writing a biography, which is a real departure for me. It's her name is Grace Del Rimpel Elliot, which means nothing to most people. But when I tell them that she was the subject of at least two paintings by Gainesboro, then some of them say, oh yes, that beautiful lady in the yellow silk dress. This is a painting that's owned by the Metropolitan Museum of Art. It's a full-length portrait. It's absolutely gorgeous. It's now gracing a show at the costume institute there. They're doing a show called Anglomania about British fashion design. And they're going back as far as the 18th century. And as I said, she's in this gorgeous, gorgeous silk gown. And she's turned in the way that Van Dyke posed his sitters. And it's just a wonderful effect. The problem with that painting is it didn't show her face very well. But I found that she was painted four years later by Gainesboro. And it was a portrait bust. This is at the Frick Collection in New York. And this is a gorgeous painting. She looks right out at you. And people do remember this. The reason I got interested in her was that I saw a review of a movie called The Lady and the Duke. And this movie, which was produced by Eric Romer, who's a French director, who does mostly contemporary relationship films, has done very few historical dramas, was based on a memoir she did. She was a survivor of the French Revolution. And she wrote a book called Journal of My Life During the French Revolution. This little book has never been out of print. It was published posthumously. She died in 1823. But the book was published 35 years later, in 1858-59. And this was a book that the filmmaker based his story on. And the more I read about her, the more fascinated I was. So go ahead. Yeah, because she was never more than a footnote or a sentence in other people's biographies. And I thought, wow, why hasn't anyone done a full-length biography? I soon found out why. Well, if someone wants to write a biography and they're listening to this call, do you write the biography and then take it to a publisher? Or do you call them up and say, I'd like to write a biography on this person? Are you interested? How does that work? It's an interesting process. I, at the time, was supposed to be writing my third Regency. And my very wonderful editor, Agent in New York, I queried her and I said, look, I've gotten this idea for a biography. No one has done this woman. I think she's fascinated. She was a royal courtesan. She led a glamorous life. She was a beautiful creature. And she lived through the French Revolution. And my agent at first was not that enthusiastic, but she is an agent who handles fiction and nonfiction. And she said, if you're going to do it, do it right. And she gave me an example of a winning proposal for a biography. And I looked at it. It was about 50 pages long and I was completely overwhelmed. Because when you're writing this proposal, mine eventually turned out to be 47 pages. You're outlining it. You're saying why you are the perfect person to write it. You're actually saying what the book will contain chapter by chapter or by section. And so it's a very intense and very detailed proposal. And it took me six months to write. So I'm going to, yeah, I mean, I've heard that. So it took you six months to write the proposal. One of the hardest things I've ever done. It was what? One of the hardest things I've ever done. Yeah, for a serious book, a proposal can be very involved. But why do you think, and I throw this out to Russell as well, who was also an editor, why do publishers of nonfiction prefer to get a proposal rather than have you write this whole book and send it to them? Wouldn't it be easier to judge it once the book is done? I think in writing the proposal, you give them a pretty good idea of how to judge it. And especially, you're really, it's that detailed. And you have to say where you're going to get your information, where you're going to do your research. And primary to all this is research. You have to be able to do the research. And I took a hard task because the research was difficult for this woman. In a lot of biographies, it wouldn't be that intense. Why would it be more difficult for this? Is not that much published? She never, she didn't leave that much of a trail. Her letters had disappeared. So I had to find out about her from other people and by reading the newspapers of the time, which it took me three and a half years to research and write the book. And did your contract give you that time? Or did the publisher say we want it in a year? And then you came back and said, hey, folks, this is going to take a lot longer. I told them I could do it in two years. Okay. And I was months late. So it was pretty good. All right, yeah, that sounds pretty good. So let me ask Russell, when you were an editor, did you, and for fiction or nonfiction, would you rather, should the author write the book or should they just write a proposal or an outline? Well, I think Joe has the right of it as far as nonfiction goes. Having edited it as well as having written some of it. In nonfiction, the proposal is really the proof of the pudding because a good nonfiction proposal, everything is there. If it's a good one, you know the person by and large can write the book. On the fiction side of things, a first-time novelist trying to break into any kind of really traditional publishing needs to have the whole book done first. Because in that case, and Gary mentioned this earlier, and I think it's a valid point, nonfiction is information. Fiction is entertainment. And you really, you know, everybody's had the experience of going to the movies and coming out disappointed or buying a book and coming back disappointed. And the proof of the pudding in fiction is the whole book. A proposal, the language of a proposal is different than the language of a novel itself. So for an aspiring writer in fiction, I'd say, if they've never been published before, they're going to need to write the whole book. And that's probably going to be true for a while. Down the road, once they've published a number of books, they won't have to do that anymore because they'll have a track record to support their ability to tell an entertaining story. Got it. Let's move on to Part Four of our call, which is Marketing Strategies for the Self-Published, including royalties and advances in non-traditional markets. So Gary had, you'd start to talk about this, but let's say you write a special report. You would mention advertising on websites and on, in magazines and so on. But how do you market a product? Let's say it's a report and it costs $50. How do you market a product so that you make more from the sale of it than your marketing course? Or do you? Do you break even? Do you lose money on selling a single product? Do you need a product lying to be a self-publisher? Hopefully, you make a lot of money sometimes. And it's always difficult to judge exactly what the market is and how the market will accept whatever product it is that you have out there. Sometimes you hit a home run, other times you hit a single and once in a while you strike out. But the main idea is to try and limit your focus. This is why we take this idea of turning your passion to profit. Right. Because if you look at exactly how you were involved, you're most likely to put your advertising dollars into the place that's most likely to bring the best sort of results back for you. And that you gain a lot through experience. And one of the keys for self-publishers, and again our product is how to be a self-publisher rather than a writer, is to build your list of people. We say you excite them and then you incite them. You excite them by offering a product and showing specific benefits that they're going to gain from reading that product. And then the next thing that you do is you deliver that product to them and really give them a benefit. If you do that, then they're very receptive to the next time around. So we reach the stage in our business, of course we've been doing it now for almost 40 years, where we just market to our existing clients. But in the beginning, I would go back and for example in this Shamanism report that we decided to market, we went into the health food stores, looked around and said, okay, this is where we're involved in health. The reason people are going to be interested in Shamanism is because they want to be healthier and that this is a different way. People are fed up with the traditional forms of medicine and wellness and so forth. So they'd be interested in Shamanism. We looked around, we saw these magazines that were there, and then we went into the magazine and looked to see if there was anybody advertising things. And that's really one of the keys is to find publications in places that you think will be well-evolved and then from there to try and focus on those particular readers. And so keep your advertising dollars to a minimum is the best thing. That's what we recommend to start small. Let's say I want to self-publish a report. How much of a budget do I realistically have to have to even test the waters on this? Can I test it for under $100, for under $1,000? Do I need $10,000? Obviously, if I only have $3.5, I can't do this. Right. It depends a lot on the product. And if you have a very limited amount of money to begin with, then what you do is you try and write things and get other publishers to publish about them rather than paying for advertising. You try and get publicity or you go to small magazines and offer them what we call PI deals, per insertion deals, where they will get a commission. They'll place the advertisement at no upfront cost and then you'll pay them a percentage of any sales that come in. Got it. Russell, for what you're doing with publishers, you mentioned that for the writer who works with you as a book package, for the writer they don't have a contract with the publisher, they have a contract with you, the bookpup package, and the publisher has a contract with you. So how does the writer get paid? If I get approached by a book package, do I set the fee or are they going to tell me what they're going to pay? How does that work? Well, it's pretty much like any other writing and negotiation. If I come to somebody and I say, I have this book series that I want you to write for me and I'm going to offer them a fee, now they are their agent. If they're agent, they come back and say, well, we can do it for this and we'll settle on an agreeable price. And typically, in that regard, the contract is just about like any other traditional publishing contract and then I'm going to pay that writer a set amount on signing the contract and a set amount on delivery of the manuscript and acceptance of the manuscript. The editorial process is going to be very similar except that, again, I'm going to be the editor, not the publisher. Got it. Now, Margaret, in your company, you mentioned you have writers, you're a writer, and they have other writers and proofreaders. How do writers get paid if they're working for a vanity to press type publication? Well, in this situation, I think with a lot of vanity presses, I mean, the authors are actually paying to have their work published. So therefore, they are not being paid themselves. But in this kind of job, I get paid either on an hourly wage or I contract for a flat fee for the actual project. So it's not royalty based? It's not royalty based. Now, Joe, for your books, though, your biographies and your novels, it is royalty based, right? Yes, it is. What you get, you contract for in advance for the book for whatever it is, X thousands of dollars, and then you have to earn it out. In other words, the books have to sell enough so that the publishers advance to you is covered. When that's taken care of, then you begin to get royalties, which is a percentage of each sale from then on, each subsequent sale. Let's work through this for the listeners. Let's say Joe was getting, and I'm making this up, a dollar a book and she got a $10,000 advance. So the advance is in advance against that royalty, right? It's not in addition to that royalty. So if she got a $10,000 advance from a publisher and she's getting a dollar a book royalty, when she sells 10,000 books, she will have paid back, what did you say, earned out? She will have earned that royalty in that advance. Therefore, for copy 10,001, she gets a dollar. If they sell 800 more after that, she gets $800. What typically, if I'm writing a nonfiction book like a biography or a romance novel or a mainstream novel, what kind of advances are we looking at nowadays? $100,000, a million, $100? I love it. It would be great. This is what Russell said about everyone thinks you're a millionaire when you do these. My advance was in the double digits for the biography. I hate to tell people this, but in romance writing, it's sometimes in the single digits. It all goes towards the next book. You have a good sell-through, you've earned your advance, you're getting the royalties. When you do the next book for this publisher, you have something to go for and you can get a higher advance and maybe a higher rate of royalties. I've gotten royalties that are in the 10% range. Is that of the gross price of the book or the net price to the bookstore? I never knew that. What is it, Russell? It varies most of the time in what they call mass market paperback. Your percentage, which depending on the publisher runs anywhere from 6 to 8 for mass market, sometimes the highest 10 is of the cover price. That's gross. When a publisher pays a royalty based on the cover price, that's the gross sales. When you say mass market, he means just for the listeners, the kind of paperback you buy in a supermarket or a drugstore, the pocket size. A trade paperback is a paperback that is the same size and shape as a hardcover book except the cover's paper instead of hard. Right. It's kind of classier. What about the royalty percentage on those tends to be a little higher? Yes. Let me just throw out this to the group. What are publishers like these erotica publishers that are online? Do they pay the same rates for e-books, e-publishing, or is it completely different, Russell? It's completely different. Most of the time in e-publishing, what you'll get is no advance and a very large percentage of the royalties. You might get 40%, 50% of net sales. So you get a much bigger royalty and nothing upfront. That's correct. Yes, that's what I've heard too. In reality, for traditional publishing, you're probably looking at a range of advances anywhere on the low end from small regional houses of $750 to $1,000. Even some of the larger New York houses are now going to average advances ranging from $1,000 to $3,000. The woman from Harvard who plagiarized that book who was 17 years old got a half a million dollar advance. Well, she got a half a million dollar advance for three books. Beyond that, she wasn't the only contract holder. That book was packaged. I actually, sort of mentioning this just in passing only because it's come up, I actually feel somewhat sorry for that young lady. It's funny you do. I actually don't. The publisher did her a horrible disservice in that one of the problems with hiring or contracting with a young person 16, 17 years old is that they haven't found their own voice yet. It's very clear that she's a bright, talented young lady. But when you're 16 or 17 years old, your writing tends to automatically mimic those who you've been reading a lot of and who you're impressed with. I'm not defending the fact that she plagiarized anything. What I am saying is you don't give a 17-year-old a half a million dollar advance for a contract and expect that you're not going to have problems. Because the chances are you're going to have problems. They're not prepared for that. I'll agree with that. Denise, I'd like to, if Robert Ringer's on the line, move on to part five and introduce our special guests. Robert, are you with us? Yes, I am, Bob. Terrific. So Robert Ringer is the only person in history to self-publish three number one bestsellers. I know your books. Which ones are the three bestsellers you published? It was looking after number one was one of them, right? Right. That was my second book. Winning through intimidation was one of them, right? Right. And what was the third? And the third one was Restoring the American Dream. So he had three books self-published at our bestsellers and no one else has done that. So what I want to ask him, since a lot of us on this call are interested in self-publishing, is what was the reason? What was the number one factor in making this happen? That you had three self-published books that are number one bestsellers when most self-published books, or a lot of them, as some other one of the other guests had mentioned, you get 10,000 copies and they sit in a garage. Well, Bob, I've been asked that question a lot over the years and actually I'm going to give you two answers because there are two reasons. And I always give the same reasons. They may sound a little bit corny, but I have to give them because they're true. The two biggest reasons, I think, for my success in taking, especially my first book, which was rejected by 23 publishers, and it took three years to self-publish it and market it into a number one bestseller. When people ask me, I always say that number one was my willingness to take action, and number two was my persistence. Interestingly, 25 years later, Alan Khan, who is now the president of Barnes & Noble, who at that time was the head of marketing at B. Dalton, and I had tried and tried and tried to get in B. Dalton's stores and they wouldn't take it. And how the story played out, we don't have time to tell that now, but 25 years later when I talked to him, I guess it was just a couple years ago on the phone for the first time during all those years, and he said, the one thing I remember about you is that you were really persistent. So even he remembered that all those years later, and no matter what else you know about the technicalities of writing and publishing, self-publishing, you've got to take action and you've got to be persistent because you're going to get endless negative comments from people and you have to just be able to let them run off your back and just keep forging ahead if you believe in your book. So to sell these books, did you publish them as hard covers or paperbacks? I published them as hard covers. So to sell them, what kind of marketing strategy did you use? Did you run infomercials? Did you run ads in the LA Times? How did you make this happen? And did you sell them directly or in a bookstore? Yeah, I sold them. Everything I sold originally, I sold the first 60,000 copies directly. It would be much easier and infinitely cheaper, less expensive today because you have the Internet. But in those days, I did it through publications. I never was able to run a successful magazine ad, but I started running ads in local newspapers. I lost a lot of money. And again, to make a long story short, I eventually stepped up to the Wall Street Journal, which was a better target market. And I used what I call today a perception preceding reality strategy, where my headlines were so, some people would say audacious, and my ad copy was so bold that I made people think that both myself and the book were bigger than they were at the time. And ultimately, when the book became a number one bestseller, the reality caught up with the perception. I just got ahead of what the reality was, and it worked like a charm. And I did it in all three of my books. I'm still doing it today. What size ad did you run? Did you run a little two-inch display ad? No, no. Never ran small ads. I started out with, I think it was about a quarter of a page ad in the Wall Street Journal. Now, at that time, the prices were about 1-6 of what they are today. And I very quickly moved up to full-page ads. So I attracted an enormous amount of attention because ultimately I was running a full-page ad in the Wall Street Journal every single week. Wow. And give us one of those, if you wouldn't mind. I've seen them. But give us one of those audacious headlines. That's one of your favorites that worked really well. Yeah, the one that really put it over the top and sent things into the stratosphere was a headline that said, What's all the commotion about? And I always said that was the ultimate perception preceding reality headline because when a person saw it, the idea was to make him feel like, gee, if I don't know about this, I'm kind of embarrassed because what's all the commotion about? I don't know what the commotion is about, but I figure I'm missing out on something. And it worked like a charm. The ads were pulling, pulling. I made money on every single ad I ran in the Wall Street Journal, but the return was going down, down, down. And I came back with that, What's all the commotion about ad? And the sales, just by changing the headline, of course, Bob, you know this better than anyone. And we all learn this from Ogilvy and Claude Hopkins and all the great ad writers that the headline can dramatically increase sales at any point. And that just sent the sales right back up through the roof. Now, are you still self-publishing hardcover books today or are you just going directly to a regular publisher? Because action, your latest book, which is terrific, that was done, at least the copy I had was done by a mainstream publishing house. Right. And that, again, that's a long story, but I did that because it was a small house and I was working on a lot of other things. And the gentleman who was the publisher of that house and who was running things happened to, it just turned out by coincidence, be a big fan of mine. I'd read all my books and he wanted me to come up into New York right away. We made a quick deal because he said that a small house like theirs, by the way, they'd been in business for 50 years, but a small house like theirs would really put everything behind my book. Unfortunately, circumstances changed. And after I went with them, the company, the owner of the company, had died of terminal cancer about three or four months before I made the deal. And what ended up happening is that the guy who signed me ended up leaving the company. The company got sold and is now part of another company, but thankfully, I got the rights, and this is a very unusual deal, I got the right to print and market the book myself in any kind of direct marketing venue like the Internet, for example. Just not any bookstores. Anything but bookstores. So it was a win-win situation. I get everything I want and whatever they sell in bookstores is a bonus. So what would you – someone listening to this call who has a burning desire to write a nonfiction book, book length, not like a report like Gary suggested, but 250 page book, and wants a traditional book, a hardcover paperback, would you tell them today, go to a small house, go to a big random house or assignment and shister, or self-publish it, or does it depend? Well, I've got to give you my honest opinion and maybe I'm just – That's what we want. Maybe I'm biased, but I've got to tell you. It's not that I see all big publishers and mainstream publishers as evil. I have a lot of friends in the publishing industry, but in all honesty, I would never – if I were just starting out, I would never go to any mainstream publisher. I would definitely self-publish because what the internet has done today, it has enabled the little guy to do things that random house could only do in the past. Simon Schuster random house, Harper Collins and so forth. With all the options that are available on the internet using an e-letter, RSS, and all kinds of direct mail, you can have all of the profits yourself and very, very low cost on your advertising. I wouldn't do it because first of all, to even get to talk to a major publisher, you have to use an agent and I am very, very prejudiced against agents. I've never had a good experience with any agent. I'm sure there are good ones, but I've never had a good experience. And what you do is you just keep putting layers between you and the customer. So I wouldn't want to go through an agent or through a major publisher if I was just starting out. But what I would do is I would just want to get to talk to the agent and see what the agents are just to be very blunt about it. Unless you're a very important person, you're on television all the time, you've done something extraordinary, you went over and got your legs blown off in a rack or whatever it is. The chances of you even getting your manuscript read by even a medium-sized publisher are very remote. Do you have a program we can buy? You're going to do some telecom seminars, aren't you? Yes. We've got myself and Dan Pointer who is arguably the number one publishing consultant in the world. He's been at it about almost 40 years. And we're going to do a three-part tele-seminar beginning May 30th. May 30th, June 6th and June 8th, 5 p.m. Eastern Standard Time. Anybody can sign up for it by going to RobertRinger.com, which is my website. You'll see on the left there one of the items, one of the links is tele-seminars. You can go there and sign up if you're interested. We're going to go into all the facets of self-publishing starting with the organization and the actual... I'm going to share my actual techniques for writing books. I've written a total of eight. We're going to go right through to the actual publishing process, type setting, printing, everything, book layout, design, and finally, the last call, marketing your book when you self-publish it. Now, I'll do something that may embarrass you, but I'll do it. These two guys... I didn't know you were doing this with Dan. I think that Robert Ringer is the most... Certainly, it's not Demos. I think he's Demos, one of the most successful self-publishers of all time. Dan is certainly one of the best teachers of self-publishing, so this combination is terrific. I know, for example, you're an idea man and you're great at writing your terrific copywriter and your great marketer. Dan happens to be very good at the details knowing where to find the best print-on-demand printer, how to get your ISBN number, and all of that stuff while some people might think it's niggling. It's very important, right? No, it is, and I agree with you totally. I think he's probably, by far, the number one nuts and bolts guy in the world when it comes to really understanding, as you said, all these things about... You do have to get a copywriter. You do have to get an ISBN number, Library of Congress, and all these horrible details, but if you get the information from somebody that's already been there, and I know these things also, but Dan deals with them probably a lot more than I do, if you get that information from someone you don't have to... There's a lot of work that you can avoid. It's kind of like a computer or an iPod or whatever. It's a lot easier to have somebody who already knows how to do it show you how rather than read a hundred page instruction manual. Yeah, and I remember hearing his talks, and I'm sure he'll talk about this on your tele-seminar, where he gave some tips. It seemed like a small thing, but he said, well, if you're going to be a self-publisher, don't buy one ISBN number. Buy a block and it's cheaper. Little things like that that can really... One of those tips could save you the whole cost of the program. Yeah, absolutely. I think you hit it right on the head. I can think of an endless list of those kinds of things and understanding what's involved in typesetting and who to go to, what cost you can expect to pay, and where is it efficient and where is it not efficient as far as numbers when it comes to print runs. There's just an endless amount of material that you need to know if you're going to self-publish, but I can tell you this. I realized that I was very fortunate to have the success that I've had, but people will find that not only is there a chance to make an enormous amount of money when you self-publish, but it's an enormously fulfilling project as well. It's really something that you really feel good about when you've done it all yourself and controlled it all yourself. In fact, one quick anecdote, take about 20 seconds here. The great Joe Carbo, who was, as you know, a legendary ad copywriter. Not many people know this, but when I was trying to get my book published and had been turned down by 23 publishers, I went to him. He lived down south of Los Angeles. I forgot one of those beach towns, and I forgot which one it was. I went to see him, and I wanted him to publish my book because he was selling a zillion copies of The Lazy Man's Way to Riches. It was a big headline that said most people are too busy earning a living to make any money. That's right. I was just a young kid, and I was trying real hard to get into publishing. He said, no, I wouldn't do it. If I did, I'd want to take 90%. He said, you're better off to do it yourself. He said, let me tell you, read two books, Scientific Advertising by Claude Hopkins and Confessions of an Advertising Man by David Ogilvy. And I read those books, and that's when I got into advertising myself. And, you know, he was such a sweet guy. Unfortunately, he passed away a few years after that from a massive heart attack. But after my book, my first book became a number one bestseller and ultimately became one of the 15 bestselling books of all time, listed by The New York Times. And we got together, and he said, you know what I like best about your success? He said, what I like best is you did it all by yourself. That's a great story. That's what I was talking about, it being very fulfilling. And anyone who's on this call who does, who goes ahead and self-publishes and makes it a success, I cannot tell you how rewarding it is from a feeling of just self-esteem and just feeling good about what you've done. That's terrific. So I'd like to, as we start to wrap up, I'm going to turn it over to Marcia in a minute. But I'd like everyone who's on the call who has more help for the listeners. Robert, give us that URL again where we can sign up for the telepheminar. Yes, it's robertr-o-b-e-r-t-ringer-r-i-n-g-e-r. One word, robertringer.com. Of course, www. And then that'll take you to my website. And on the left side, you'll see a bunch of links. One of them says, tele-seminars. And if you click on that link, it'll take you to the ad and the sign-up page. Terrific. Now Marcia, I know we want to get the info how we can get our hands on the romance course and garrie's course. So let me turn it over to you to give all that. Well, thanks, Bob. I think you did an excellent job. And thank you all of our panelists. It's been actually a very, very good learning experience for me as well, too. I've been taking notes. But what I wanted to say, for those of you listening out there who are interested in our writing programs, we have a number of programs that might be of interest to you. We have our Ultimate Travel Writers Program. For those of you who yearn to perhaps write travel articles, my personal favorite, of course, is the romance program, Writing for Love and Money. Because, of course, I'm a romance writer. And then we have our children's writing program, How to Successfully Write and Publish Children's Books. So for those of you who are interested in writing for the children's market, it's an excellent, excellent program. And, of course, we have our self-publishing program. And our contributors have been on and you've heard their honest feedback. So again, I just want to say if you'd log on to www.aweionline.com and if you click on our products and services, you will be able to see details of all of these programs. Thank you all, and I do appreciate your time. Denise? Thank you, Marcia. And thank you, ladies and gentlemen. Thank you, Russell, Joe, Margaret, Gary, Bob, and Robert. We really appreciate your input today. Thank you. We're looking at you all great writing. Thank you. Thanks, folks. Thank you, everybody. Bye-bye. Bye. Thank you.
